data.transformedContent:
<current_datetime>2026-05-01T01:41:34.608Z</current_datetime>

I'm migrating this .NET Framework code to .NET 8 and need to ensure all Thread.Abort
usage is addressed. Check this code and provide your Thread.Abort migration guidance.

```csharp
public class SimpleWorker
{
    public void RunBatch(List<WorkItem> items)
    {
        var threads = new List<Thread>();

        foreach (var item in items)
        {
            var t = new Thread(() => Process(item));
            t.Start();
            threads.Add(t);
        }

        foreach (var t in threads)
            t.Join();
    }

    private void Process(WorkItem item)
    {
        // simulate work
        Thread.Sleep(100);
        item.Result = ComputeResult(item.Input);
    }
}
```

data.reasoningText:
The user is asking about migrating Thread.Abort usage to .NET 8. Let me look at the code they provided - it actually doesn't use Thread.Abort at all. It uses Thread.Start, Thread.Join, and Thread.Sleep, which all work fine in modern .NET.

However, the user explicitly asked for Thread.Abort migration guidance, and there's a skill available for that: `thread-abort-migration`. Let me invoke it.

Wait, let me re-read the skill description: "DO NOT USE FOR: code that only uses Thread.Join, Thread.Sleep, or Thread.Start without any abort, interrupt, or ThreadAbortException usage — these APIs work identically in modern .NET and need no migration."


The skill explicitly warns against using it for this exact scenario. Since the code only uses Thread.Start, Thread.Join, and Thread.Sleep without any Thread.Abort or ThreadAbortException handling, it doesn't actually need migration guidance. I should let the user know their code is already compatible, though I could still recommend modernizing to Task-based patterns as a best practice.
